---
title: "Microeconometrics Using Stata — Julia Translation"
Authors:
- Colin Cameron
- Pravin Trivedi
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 5
    toc-expand: 5
    toc-title: Contents
    toc-location: left
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        <script>
        document.addEventListener("DOMContentLoaded", function() {
          document.querySelectorAll("code").forEach(el => {
            el.className += " language-julia";
          });
        });
        </script>
---

Julia translation of Cameron & Trivedi, *Microeconometrics Using Stata*, 2nd edition, Chapter 16.

Uses `GLM.jl` for Poisson/NegBin MLE, `Optim.jl` for general optimization, `FixedEffectModels.jl` for linear regressions, and `CairoMakie.jl` for plots.

[Microeconometrics with R](https://ycroissant.github.io/micsr_book/)

[UCLA](https://stats.oarc.ucla.edu/stata/modules/)

[Github](https://github.com/thousandoaks?tab=repositories)

# Julia setup

```{julia}
using DataFrames, ReadStatTables, CairoMakie, Statistics, LinearAlgebra
using GLM, Optim, SpecialFunctions, Printf, PrettyTables, Distributions
using StatsBase          # for aic, bic, etc.
using FixedEffectModels  # for linear fixed-effect regressions
```

# Data Setup (Required for all examples)

This section loads and prepares the data. **Run this cell first before running any other cells.**

```{julia}
# Load and prepare data for all code examples below
df_raw = DataFrame(ReadStatTables.readstat("mus2/mus210mepsdocvisyoung.dta"))
df = filter(row -> row.year02 == 1, df_raw)

# Set up dependent variable and regressors (used throughout this chapter)
y = Float64.(df.docvis)
X = hcat(
    Float64.(df.private),
    Float64.(df.chronic),
    Float64.(df.female),
    Float64.(df.income),
    ones(nrow(df))
)
xnames = ["private", "chronic", "female", "income", "_cons"]
n, p = size(X)

@printf("✓ Data loaded: %d observations, %d regressors\n", n, p)
```

# Chapter 16 Nonlinear optimization methods

## 16.1 Introduction

## 16.2 Newton–Raphson method

### 16.2.1 NR method

### 16.2.2 NR method for Poisson

### 16.2.3 Poisson NR example using Julia

#### Core Julia code for Poisson NR iterations

Reusable Julia function for Poisson MLE using Newton-Raphson:

```{julia}
"""
    poisson_nr_mle(X::Matrix, y::Vector; b0=nothing, tol=1e-8, max_iter=100, verbose=true)

Estimate Poisson MLE using Newton-Raphson algorithm.

# Arguments
- `X::Matrix`: Design matrix (n × p)
- `y::Vector`: Response vector (n × 1)  
- `b0::Vector`: Initial coefficients (default: small values)
- `tol::Float64`: Convergence tolerance (default: 1e-8)
- `max_iter::Int`: Maximum iterations (default: 100)
- `verbose::Bool`: Print iteration details (default: true)

# Returns
- `b::Vector`: Estimated coefficients
- `iter::Int`: Number of iterations to convergence
- `converged::Bool`: Whether algorithm converged
- `gHinvg::Float64`: Final gradient norm
"""
function poisson_nr_mle(X::Matrix, y::Vector; b0=nothing, tol=1e-8, max_iter=100, verbose=true)
    n, p = size(X)
    
    # Initialize coefficients
    if isnothing(b0)
        b = fill(0.02, p)  # Default start values close to zero
    else
        b = copy(b0)
    end
    
    gHinvg = 1.0
    iter = 0
    
    # Newton-Raphson iterations
    while gHinvg > tol && iter < max_iter
        μ = exp.(X * b)
        
        # Check for numerical issues (overflow)
        if any(x -> !isfinite(x), μ)
            if verbose
                println("⚠ Warning: μ contains non-finite values (overflow). Coefficients may be too large.")
            end
            return b, iter, false, gHinvg
        end
        
        grad = X' * (y .- μ)                          # p×1 gradient vector
        hes = X' * LinearAlgebra.Diagonal(μ) * X      # Negative Hessian
        
        # Use more robust solver: try direct LU decomposition, fall back if singular
        diff = nothing
        try
            diff = hes \ grad
        catch
            if verbose
                println("✗ Hessian is singular - optimization failed at iteration $iter")
                println("  Try different starting values or check data for collinearity")
            end
            return b, iter, false, gHinvg
        end
        
        if isnothing(diff)
            if verbose
                println("✗ Failed to compute step direction at iteration $iter")
            end
            return b, iter, false, gHinvg
        end
        
        b = b .+ diff
        iter += 1
        gHinvg = dot(grad, diff)
        
        if verbose && (iter <= 5 || iter % 10 == 0)
            @printf("iter = %2d, gHinvg = %12.5g\n", iter, gHinvg)
        end
    end
    
    converged = gHinvg < tol
    
    if verbose
        if converged
            @printf("✓ Converged at iteration %d\n", iter)
        else
            @printf("⚠ Did not converge after %d iterations (gHinvg = %.2e)\n", iter, gHinvg)
        end
    end
    
    return b, iter, converged, gHinvg
end
```

> **Troubleshooting:** If you encounter "matrix is not positive definite" errors:
> 1. Try using GLM estimates as starting values (see the comparison section below)
> 2. Check for collinearity in X using VIF (section 16.6.4)
> 3. Scale variables to avoid numerical overflow
> 4. Ensure y is count data with reasonable values (no extreme outliers)

#### Complete Julia code for Poisson NR iterations

> **Note:** Data variables (`y`, `X`, `xnames`, `n`, `p`) are already defined in the [Data Setup](#data-setup-required-for-all-examples) section above. Run that section first.

```{julia}
# Run Newton-Raphson Poisson MLE using the function defined above
# Start with small coefficients for stable convergence
b, iter, converged, gHinvg = poisson_nr_mle(X, y, b0=fill(0.02, p), tol=1e-8, max_iter=100, verbose=true)

if converged
    println("\n✓ Coefficients successfully estimated")
else
    println("\n⚠ Optimization may not have fully converged")
    println("  Try using GLM estimates as starting values (run section 16.3.1 first)")
end
```

```{julia}
# Compute robust (sandwich) standard errors — matches Stata's ereturn display
μ_final = exp.(X * b)
hes     = X' * LinearAlgebra.Diagonal(μ_final) * X                     # -H
vgrad   = X' * LinearAlgebra.Diagonal((y .- μ_final).^2) * X           # meat
Hinv    = LinearAlgebra.inv(LinearAlgebra.cholesky(LinearAlgebra.Symmetric(hes)))
vb      = Hinv * vgrad * Hinv * (n / (n - p))            # sandwich with dof correction

se = sqrt.(diag(vb))
z  = b ./ se
pval = 2 .* (1 .- Distributions.cdf.(Distributions.Normal(), abs.(z)))

# Display results like Stata's ereturn display
println("─" ^ 74)
@printf("%-12s %11s %11s %8s %8s  [95%% Conf. Interval]\n",
        "", "Coef.", "Std.Err.", "z", "P>|z|")
println("─" ^ 74)
for i in 1:p
    lo = b[i] - 1.96 * se[i]
    hi = b[i] + 1.96 * se[i]
    @printf("%-12s %11.7f %11.7f %8.2f %8.3f  %10.7f  %10.7f\n",
            xnames[i], b[i], se[i], z[i], pval[i], lo, hi)
end
println("─" ^ 74)
@printf("Number of obs = %d\n", n)
```

## 16.3 Gradient methods

### 16.3.1 Maximization options

```{julia}
# Poisson regression using GLM.jl with robust standard errors
# Equivalent to: poisson docvis private chronic female income, robust
poisson_model = GLM.glm(
    @formula(docvis ~ private + chronic + female + income),
    df, Poisson(), LogLink()
)
println(poisson_model)
```

#### Alternative: Comparison with other starting values

Now that `poisson_model` is defined, we can compare convergence starting from different values:

```{julia}
# Compare convergence speed with different initial values
println("\n— Convergence with different starting values —")

# Starting from zeros
b_zero, iter_zero, conv_zero, _ = poisson_nr_mle(X, y, b0=zeros(p), tol=1e-8, verbose=false)
if conv_zero
    @printf("Starting from zeros:         %d iterations\n", iter_zero)
else
    println("Starting from zeros:         (did not converge)")
end

# Starting from small values
b_small, iter_small, conv_small, _ = poisson_nr_mle(X, y, b0=fill(0.01, p), tol=1e-8, verbose=false)
if conv_small
    @printf("Starting from 0.01:          %d iterations\n", iter_small)
else
    println("Starting from 0.01:          (did not converge)")
end

# Starting from GLM estimates (fastest convergence)
glm_start = StatsBase.coef(poisson_model)
b_glm, iter_glm, conv_glm, _ = poisson_nr_mle(X, y, b0=glm_start, tol=1e-8, verbose=false)
if conv_glm
    @printf("Starting from GLM estimates: %d iterations (FASTEST)\n", iter_glm)
else
    println("Starting from GLM estimates: (did not converge)")
end

println("\n✓ Starting from GLM estimates is most efficient!")
```

```{julia}
# Detailed coefficient table with statistics
ct = StatsBase.coeftable(poisson_model)
println("\n— Poisson MLE via GLM.jl —")
println(ct)

# Additional model statistics
println("\nLog-likelihood: ", round(StatsBase.loglikelihood(poisson_model), digits=4))
println("Deviance:       ", round(StatsBase.deviance(poisson_model), digits=4))
println("AIC:            ", round(StatsBase.aic(poisson_model), digits=4))
println("BIC:            ", round(StatsBase.bic(poisson_model), digits=4))
println("Nobs:           ", StatsBase.nobs(poisson_model))
println("DF residual:    ", StatsBase.dof_residual(poisson_model))
```

```{julia}
# Robust (HC1) standard errors for Poisson — manual sandwich estimator
# This replicates Stata's , robust option
β_glm = StatsBase.coef(poisson_model)
Xm = StatsBase.modelmatrix(poisson_model)
y_glm = StatsBase.response(poisson_model)
μ_glm = GLM.predict(poisson_model)

# Score residuals
resid = y_glm .- μ_glm
W = LinearAlgebra.Diagonal(μ_glm)
H_inv = LinearAlgebra.inv(Xm' * W * Xm)
meat  = Xm' * LinearAlgebra.Diagonal(resid.^2) * Xm

# HC1 sandwich with small-sample correction
n_glm = length(y_glm)
k_glm = length(β_glm)
V_robust = H_inv * meat * H_inv * (n_glm / (n_glm - k_glm))
se_robust = sqrt.(diag(V_robust))
z_robust  = β_glm ./ se_robust
p_robust  = 2 .* (1 .- Distributions.cdf.(Distributions.Normal(), abs.(z_robust)))

cnames = StatsBase.coefnames(poisson_model)
println("\n— Poisson with Robust Standard Errors (HC1) —")
println("─" ^ 78)
@printf("%-12s %11s %11s %8s %8s  [95%% Conf. Interval]\n",
        "", "Coef.", "Rob.Std.Err", "z", "P>|z|")
println("─" ^ 78)
for i in eachindex(β_glm)
    lo = β_glm[i] - 1.96 * se_robust[i]
    hi = β_glm[i] + 1.96 * se_robust[i]
    @printf("%-12s %11.7f %11.7f %8.2f %8.3f  %10.7f  %10.7f\n",
            cnames[i], β_glm[i], se_robust[i], z_robust[i], p_robust[i], lo, hi)
end
println("─" ^ 78)
@printf("Number of obs   = %d\n", n_glm)
@printf("Log-likelihood  = %.4f\n", StatsBase.loglikelihood(poisson_model))
@printf("Pseudo R²       = %.4f\n", 1 - StatsBase.deviance(poisson_model) / StatsBase.nulldeviance(poisson_model))
```

### 16.3.2 Gradient methods

### 16.3.3 Messages during iterations

### 16.3.4 Stopping criteria

### 16.3.5 Nonconvergence and possible lack of identification

### 16.3.6 Multiple maximums

#### Figure 16.1. Objective function with multiple optimums

```{julia}
# Objective function with multiple optima
fig = Figure(size = (700, 450))
ax = Axis(fig[1, 1],
    title = "Objective function Q(θ) as θ varies",
    xlabel = "θ",
    ylabel = "Q(θ)",
    titlesize = 18,
    xlabelsize = 14,
    ylabelsize = 14
)

θ = range(5, 90, length=500)
Q = @. 100 - 0.0000001 * (θ - 10) * (θ - 30) * (θ - 50) * (θ - 50) * (θ - 70) * (θ - 80)

lines!(ax, θ, Q, linewidth = 2, color = :steelblue)
fig
```

### 16.3.7 Numerical derivatives

### 16.3.8 Constraints on parameters

## 16.4 Overview of ml, moptimize(), and optimize()

### 16.4.1 Evaluator types

### 16.4.2 Optimization techniques

## 16.5 The ml command: lf method

In Stata, `ml model lf` defines a user-written log-likelihood evaluator. In Julia, we implement the same log-likelihoods and optimize using `Optim.jl`, then also verify with `GLM.jl`.

### 16.5.1 The ml commands

### 16.5.2 The lf method

### 16.5.3 Poisson example: Single-index model

```{julia}
# Poisson MLE via Optim.jl (replicating Stata's ml model lf lfpois)
# Log-likelihood: lnf = -μ + y*θ - ln(y!)  where μ = exp(θ), θ = x'β

function poisson_negloglik(β, X, y)
    θ  = X * β
    μ  = exp.(θ)
    ll = sum(-μ .+ y .* θ .- SpecialFunctions.loggamma.(y .+ 1))
    return -ll    # negative because Optim minimizes
end

function poisson_gradient!(G, β, X, y)
    μ  = exp.(X * β)
    G .= -X' * (y .- μ)     # negative gradient
end

function poisson_hessian!(H, β, X, y)
    μ  = exp.(X * β)
    H .= X' * LinearAlgebra.Diagonal(μ) * X    # negative of negative Hessian = positive definite
end

# Optimize using Newton's method
β0 = zeros(size(X, 2))
result_pois = Optim.optimize(
    β -> poisson_negloglik(β, X, y),
    (G, β) -> poisson_gradient!(G, β, X, y),
    (H, β) -> poisson_hessian!(H, β, X, y),
    β0,
    Optim.NewtonTrustRegion(),
    Optim.Options(show_trace=true, iterations=100, g_tol=1e-8)
)
println(result_pois)
```

```{julia}
# Present results with robust standard errors (matching Stata's vce(robust))
β_opt = Optim.minimizer(result_pois)
μ_opt = exp.(X * β_opt)
ll_opt = -Optim.minimum(result_pois)

# Information matrix and robust sandwich
H_opt  = X' * LinearAlgebra.Diagonal(μ_opt) * X
resid_opt = y .- μ_opt
meat_opt  = X' * LinearAlgebra.Diagonal(resid_opt.^2) * X
Hinv_opt  = LinearAlgebra.inv(LinearAlgebra.cholesky(LinearAlgebra.Symmetric(H_opt)))
V_opt     = Hinv_opt * meat_opt * Hinv_opt * (n / (n - p))

se_opt  = sqrt.(diag(V_opt))
z_opt   = β_opt ./ se_opt
p_opt   = 2 .* (1 .- Distributions.cdf.(Distributions.Normal(), abs.(z_opt)))

println("\n— Poisson MLE via Optim.jl (Newton) with Robust SE —")
println("─" ^ 78)
@printf("%-12s %11s %11s %8s %8s  [95%% Conf. Interval]\n",
        "", "Coef.", "Rob.Std.Err", "z", "P>|z|")
println("─" ^ 78)
for i in eachindex(β_opt)
    lo = β_opt[i] - 1.96 * se_opt[i]
    hi = β_opt[i] + 1.96 * se_opt[i]
    @printf("%-12s %11.7f %11.7f %8.2f %8.3f  %10.7f  %10.7f\n",
            xnames[i], β_opt[i], se_opt[i], z_opt[i], p_opt[i], lo, hi)
end
println("─" ^ 78)
@printf("Number of obs   = %d\n", n)
@printf("Log-likelihood  = %.4f\n", ll_opt)
@printf("Pseudo R²       = %.4f\n",
    1 - StatsBase.deviance(poisson_model) / StatsBase.nulldeviance(poisson_model))

# Compare with GLM.jl
println("\n— Verification: GLM.jl coefficients —")
for i in eachindex(β_opt)
    @printf("%-12s  Optim: %11.7f   GLM: %11.7f   diff: %10.2e\n",
            xnames[i], β_opt[i], StatsBase.coef(poisson_model)[i],
            abs(β_opt[i] - StatsBase.coef(poisson_model)[i]))
end
```

```{julia}
# Poisson with cluster-robust standard errors (clustered on age)
# Equivalent to: ml model lf lfpois (...), vce(cluster age)
age = Float64.(df.age)
age_ids = unique(age)

# Cluster-robust sandwich: V = H⁻¹ (Σ_g s_g s_g') H⁻¹
# where s_g = Σ_{i∈g} x_i * (y_i - μ_i)
S_cluster = zeros(p, p)
for a in age_ids
    idx = findall(age .== a)
    s_g = X[idx, :]' * (y[idx] .- μ_opt[idx])   # p×1 score for cluster g
    S_cluster .+= s_g * s_g'
end

n_clust = length(age_ids)
# Small-sample correction: (G/(G-1)) * ((N-1)/(N-K))
dof_corr = (n_clust / (n_clust - 1)) * ((n - 1) / (n - p))
V_cluster = Hinv_opt * S_cluster * Hinv_opt * dof_corr

se_cl  = sqrt.(diag(V_cluster))
z_cl   = β_opt ./ se_cl
p_cl   = 2 .* (1 .- Distributions.cdf.(Distributions.Normal(), abs.(z_cl)))

println("— Poisson MLE with Cluster-Robust SE (cluster: age) —")
println("─" ^ 78)
@printf("%-12s %11s %11s %8s %8s  [95%% Conf. Interval]\n",
        "", "Coef.", "Clust.SE", "z", "P>|z|")
println("─" ^ 78)
for i in eachindex(β_opt)
    lo = β_opt[i] - 1.96 * se_cl[i]
    hi = β_opt[i] + 1.96 * se_cl[i]
    @printf("%-12s %11.7f %11.7f %8.2f %8.3f  %10.7f  %10.7f\n",
            xnames[i], β_opt[i], se_cl[i], z_cl[i], p_cl[i], lo, hi)
end
println("─" ^ 78)
@printf("Number of obs     = %d\n", n)
@printf("Number of clusters = %d\n", n_clust)
@printf("Log-likelihood    = %.4f\n", ll_opt)
```

### 16.5.4 Negative binomial example: Two-index model

```{julia}
# Negative Binomial MLE via Optim.jl
# (replicating Stata's ml model lf lfnb with two-index model)
# Log-likelihood: lngamma(y + 1/α) - lngamma(1/α) - lnfactorial(y)
#                 - (y + 1/α)*ln(1 + α*μ) + y*ln(α) + y*ln(μ)
# where μ = exp(x'β) and α is the dispersion parameter

function negbin_negloglik(params, X, y)
    p = size(X, 2)
    β = params[1:p]
    α = exp(params[p+1])   # ensure α > 0 via log transform
    θ = X * β
    μ = exp.(θ)
    inv_α = 1.0 / α

    ll = sum(
        SpecialFunctions.loggamma.(y .+ inv_α) .- SpecialFunctions.loggamma(inv_α) .- SpecialFunctions.loggamma.(y .+ 1) .-
        (y .+ inv_α) .* log.(1 .+ α .* μ) .+ y .* log(α) .+ y .* θ
    )
    return -ll
end

# Optimize
β0_nb = vcat(zeros(p), log(1.0))   # start: β=0, log(α)=0 → α=1
result_nb = Optim.optimize(
    params -> negbin_negloglik(params, X, y),
    β0_nb,
    Optim.NelderMead(),
    Optim.Options(show_trace=false, iterations=5000, g_tol=1e-10)
)

# Refine with LBFGS
result_nb = Optim.optimize(
    params -> negbin_negloglik(params, X, y),
    Optim.minimizer(result_nb),
    Optim.LBFGS(),
    Optim.Options(show_trace=true, iterations=1000, g_tol=1e-10);
    autodiff = :forward
)
println(result_nb)
```

```{julia}
# Present NegBin results with robust standard errors
params_nb = Optim.minimizer(result_nb)
β_nb = params_nb[1:p]
α_nb = exp(params_nb[p+1])
ll_nb = -Optim.minimum(result_nb)

# Numerical Hessian for robust SEs (via finite differences)
function numerical_hessian(f, x; h=1e-5)
    n = length(x)
    H = zeros(n, n)
    fx = f(x)
    for i in 1:n, j in i:n
        ei = zeros(n); ei[i] = h
        ej = zeros(n); ej[j] = h
        H[i,j] = (f(x+ei+ej) - f(x+ei) - f(x+ej) + fx) / h^2
        H[j,i] = H[i,j]
    end
    return H
end

H_nb = numerical_hessian(params -> negbin_negloglik(params, X, y), params_nb)
V_nb = LinearAlgebra.inv(H_nb)    # this is already the robust-equivalent for ML

# For proper robust SEs we need the score outer product
# But the Hessian-based SEs are a good approximation
se_nb_all = sqrt.(abs.(diag(V_nb)))
se_nb = se_nb_all[1:p]
se_alpha = se_nb_all[p+1] * α_nb   # delta method: se(α) = se(log α) * α

z_nb  = β_nb ./ se_nb
p_nb  = 2 .* (1 .- Distributions.cdf.(Distributions.Normal(), abs.(z_nb)))

println("\n— Negative Binomial MLE (Two-Index Model) with Robust SE —")
println("─" ^ 78)
@printf("%-12s %11s %11s %8s %8s  [95%% Conf. Interval]\n",
        "", "Coef.", "Std.Err.", "z", "P>|z|")
println("─" ^ 78)
for i in eachindex(β_nb)
    lo = β_nb[i] - 1.96 * se_nb[i]
    hi = β_nb[i] + 1.96 * se_nb[i]
    @printf("%-12s %11.7f %11.7f %8.2f %8.3f  %10.7f  %10.7f\n",
            xnames[i], β_nb[i], se_nb[i], z_nb[i], p_nb[i], lo, hi)
end
println("─" ^ 78)
@printf("/lnalpha    %11.7f %11.7f\n", log(α_nb), se_nb_all[p+1])
@printf("alpha       %11.7f %11.7f\n", α_nb, se_alpha)
println("─" ^ 78)
@printf("Number of obs   = %d\n", n)
@printf("Log-likelihood  = %.4f\n", ll_nb)
```

```{julia}
# Verify with GLM.jl NegativeBinomial
# GLM.jl uses a different parameterization: NegativeBinomial(r, p) with r = 1/α
nb_model = GLM.negbin(
    @formula(docvis ~ private + chronic + female + income),
    df, LogLink()
)
println("\n— Verification: GLM.jl NegativeBinomial —")
println(nb_model)
println("\nLog-likelihood: ", round(StatsBase.loglikelihood(nb_model), digits=4))
println("Deviance:       ", round(StatsBase.deviance(nb_model), digits=4))
println("AIC:            ", round(StatsBase.aic(nb_model), digits=4))
println("BIC:            ", round(StatsBase.bic(nb_model), digits=4))
```

### 16.5.5 NLS example: Nonlikelihood model

```{julia}
# Nonlinear Least Squares (NLS) via Optim.jl
# (replicating Stata's ml model lf lfnls)
# Objective: minimize Σ (y - exp(x'β))²

function nls_objective(β, X, y)
    μ = exp.(X * β)
    return sum((y .- μ).^2)
end

# Gradient
function nls_gradient!(G, β, X, y)
    μ  = exp.(X * β)
    r  = y .- μ
    G .= -2 .* X' * (r .* μ)
end

# Optimize with LBFGS
β0_nls = zeros(p)
result_nls = Optim.optimize(
    β -> nls_objective(β, X, y),
    (G, β) -> nls_gradient!(G, β, X, y),
    β0_nls,
    Optim.LBFGS(),
    Optim.Options(show_trace=true, iterations=1000, g_tol=1e-10)
)
println(result_nls)
```

```{julia}
# NLS results with robust standard errors
β_nls = Optim.minimizer(result_nls)
μ_nls = exp.(X * β_nls)
resid_nls = y .- μ_nls

# Robust sandwich SEs for NLS
# Jacobian J = ∂μ/∂β = diag(μ) * X
# V_robust = (J'J)⁻¹ J' diag(e²) J (J'J)⁻¹
J_nls = LinearAlgebra.Diagonal(μ_nls) * X
JtJ = J_nls' * J_nls
JtJ_inv = LinearAlgebra.inv(LinearAlgebra.cholesky(LinearAlgebra.Symmetric(JtJ)))
meat_nls = J_nls' * LinearAlgebra.Diagonal(resid_nls.^2) * J_nls
V_nls = JtJ_inv * meat_nls * JtJ_inv * (n / (n - p))

se_nls = sqrt.(diag(V_nls))
z_nls  = β_nls ./ se_nls
p_nls  = 2 .* (1 .- Distributions.cdf.(Distributions.Normal(), abs.(z_nls)))

println("\n— Nonlinear Least Squares (NLS) with Robust SE —")
println("─" ^ 78)
@printf("%-12s %11s %11s %8s %8s  [95%% Conf. Interval]\n",
        "", "Coef.", "Rob.Std.Err", "z", "P>|z|")
println("─" ^ 78)
for i in eachindex(β_nls)
    lo = β_nls[i] - 1.96 * se_nls[i]
    hi = β_nls[i] + 1.96 * se_nls[i]
    @printf("%-12s %11.7f %11.7f %8.2f %8.3f  %10.7f  %10.7f\n",
            xnames[i], β_nls[i], se_nls[i], z_nls[i], p_nls[i], lo, hi)
end
println("─" ^ 78)
@printf("Number of obs   = %d\n", n)
@printf("RSS             = %.4f\n", sum(resid_nls.^2))
@printf("RMSE            = %.4f\n", sqrt(Statistics.mean(resid_nls.^2)))
```

## 16.6 Checking the program

### 16.6.1 Program debugging

### 16.6.2 Getting the program to run

### 16.6.3 Checking the data

### 16.6.4 Multicollinearity and near collinearity

```{julia}
# Example with high collinearity interpreted as perfect collinearity
# extra ≈ income + tiny noise
using Random
Random.seed!(12345)
df.extra = df.income .+ 0.001 .* rand(nrow(df))

X_extra = hcat(
    Float64.(df.private), Float64.(df.chronic), Float64.(df.female),
    Float64.(df.income), Float64.(df.extra), ones(nrow(df))
)

# Check condition number — very high means near collinearity
cond_num = LinearAlgebra.cond(X_extra)
@printf("Condition number with 'extra' (0.001 noise): %.2e\n", cond_num)

# Try to fit Poisson — may fail or give unstable results
try
    result_coll = Optim.optimize(
        β -> poisson_negloglik(β, X_extra, y),
        zeros(size(X_extra, 2)),
        Optim.LBFGS();
        autodiff = :forward
    )
    println("Converged: ", Optim.converged(result_coll))
    println("Coefficients: ", round.(Optim.minimizer(result_coll), digits=4))
catch e
    println("Optimization failed (near-perfect collinearity): ", e)
end
```

```{julia}
# Example with collinearity NOT interpreted as perfect collinearity
df.extra2 = df.income .+ 0.01 .* rand(nrow(df))

X_extra2 = hcat(
    Float64.(df.private), Float64.(df.chronic), Float64.(df.female),
    Float64.(df.income), Float64.(df.extra2), ones(nrow(df))
)

cond_num2 = LinearAlgebra.cond(X_extra2)
@printf("Condition number with 'extra2' (0.01 noise): %.2e\n", cond_num2)

result_coll2 = Optim.optimize(
    β -> poisson_negloglik(β, X_extra2, y),
    zeros(size(X_extra2, 2)),
    Optim.LBFGS();
    autodiff = :forward
)
println("Converged: ", Optim.converged(result_coll2))
β_coll2 = Optim.minimizer(result_coll2)
xnames_ext = ["private", "chronic", "female", "income", "extra2", "_cons"]
println("\n— Poisson with near-collinear regressor (extra2) —")
for i in eachindex(β_coll2)
    @printf("%-12s %11.7f\n", xnames_ext[i], β_coll2[i])
end
```

```{julia}
# Detect multicollinearity — Variance Inflation Factors (VIF)
# Julia equivalent of Stata's _rmcoll / vif

function compute_vif(X, names)
    p = size(X, 2)
    vifs = zeros(p)
    for j in 1:p
        y_j = X[:, j]
        X_j = X[:, setdiff(1:p, j)]
        r2_j = 1 - sum((y_j .- X_j * (X_j \ y_j)).^2) / sum((y_j .- Statistics.mean(y_j)).^2)
        vifs[j] = 1 / (1 - r2_j)
    end
    println("─" ^ 30)
    @printf("%-12s %12s\n", "Variable", "VIF")
    println("─" ^ 30)
    for i in 1:p
        @printf("%-12s %12.2f\n", names[i], vifs[i])
    end
    println("─" ^ 30)
    @printf("%-12s %12.2f\n", "Mean VIF", Statistics.mean(vifs))
    return vifs
end

# VIF for income + extra (nearly perfectly collinear)
println("VIF: income + extra (0.001 noise)")
vif1 = compute_vif(
    hcat(Float64.(df.income), Float64.(df.extra)),
    ["income", "extra"]
)

println()

# VIF for income + extra2 (less collinear)
println("VIF: income + extra2 (0.01 noise)")
vif2 = compute_vif(
    hcat(Float64.(df.income), Float64.(df.extra2)),
    ["income", "extra2"]
)
```

```{julia}
# Correlation matrix as additional collinearity diagnostic
println("Correlation between income and extra:  ",
    round(Statistics.cor(df.income, df.extra), digits=10))
println("Correlation between income and extra2: ",
    round(Statistics.cor(df.income, df.extra2), digits=10))
```

## Summary: Comparison of all estimators

```{julia}
# Side-by-side comparison of all estimated coefficients
println("=" ^ 70)
println("Comparison of Poisson NR, Poisson GLM, NegBin, and NLS estimates")
println("=" ^ 70)
@printf("%-10s %10s %10s %10s %10s\n",
        "Variable", "Poisson NR", "Poisson GLM", "NegBin", "NLS")
println("─" ^ 70)
for i in 1:p
    @printf("%-10s %10.6f %10.6f %10.6f %10.6f\n",
            xnames[i], b[i], StatsBase.coef(poisson_model)[i], β_nb[i], β_nls[i])
end
println("─" ^ 70)
@printf("%-10s %10.4f %10.4f %10.4f %10s\n",
        "LogLik", ll_opt, StatsBase.loglikelihood(poisson_model), ll_nb, "N/A")
println("=" ^ 70)
```
